title: Rewrite ALL 34 DS descriptions with hook + intuition + formal structure
description:
Every DS description currently opens with a dry definition ('Contiguous block of memory...', 'Binary Search Tree: left child < parent < right child...'). Zero hooks, zero analogies, zero 'why should I care.' ALL 34 score F on T1 (Hook) and T2 (Intuition).

Rewrite each description following this template:
1. HOOK (1 sentence): Why should I care? Real problem or real system.
2. INTUITION (2-3 sentences): Analogy that builds a mental model.
3. FORMAL (1-2 sentences): Precise definition.
4. WHEN TO USE (1 sentence): When to pick this over alternatives.
5. COMPLEXITY FEEL (1 sentence): What the O notation means concretely.

Example rewrites provided in the teaching quality audit:
- Array: 'Mailbox row' analogy
- Hash Table: 'Library catalog' analogy
- BST: 'Phone book binary search' analogy
- Linked List: 'Scavenger hunt' analogy
- Heap: 'ER triage' analogy
- Bloom Filter: 'Chrome Safe Browsing' hook

Sources for best analogies: AlgoCademy blog, Brilliant.org, Medium 'explained simply' articles, Cornell CS 2110.
acceptanceCriteria: Every DS description starts with a hook sentence (WHY it matters)
Every description includes an intuitive analogy or concrete example
Every description includes a 'When to use' guidance sentence
Every description includes a complexity intuition sentence
Descriptions are 4-6 sentences, not walls of text
A beginner reading only the description could explain the DS to a friend

title: Add WHY reasoning to step descriptions in all 34 engine files
description:
ALL 34 topics score 1/3 on T4 (Step WHY). Steps say WHAT happens ('Compare 42 with 25', 'Go right', 'Swap A and B') but never WHY.

For each engine, augment key steps with reasoning:

BST example:
  Current: 'Compare 42 with 25'
  Better: 'Compare 42 with node 25. In a BST, values greater than a node go right. Since 42 > 25, we move right — eliminating the entire left subtree.'

AVL example:
  Current: 'LL case at 25: bf=2'
  Better: 'Node 25 is unbalanced (left subtree is 2 levels taller). This LL case means the heavy side is the left child\'s left subtree. A single right rotation will fix it.'

Hash Table example:
  Current: 'hash("alice") = 3'
  Better: 'We hash "alice" to get bucket index 3. The hash function converts any key into a number within our table size, so we know exactly WHERE to look.'

Prioritize: The first 2-3 steps of each operation are most important — that's when the student is building their mental model. Later steps can be more concise.

Target: 50%+ of steps in each engine should include WHY reasoning.
acceptanceCriteria: First step of every operation includes WHY reasoning
Key decision steps (go left/right, swap, rotate) explain the logic
Comparison steps connect to the data structure's fundamental property
AVL/RB rotation steps explain what was unbalanced and why the rotation fixes it
At least 50% of steps include WHY, not just WHAT

title: Add complexity intuition text that explains what O notation MEANS
description:
ALL topics show complexity as raw notation ('O(n)', 'O(log n)', 'O(1)') with zero explanation. Students memorize O(n log n) without understanding what it MEANS.

For each DS, add a 'Complexity Intuition' paragraph in the properties panel:

  Array Access O(1): 'Accessing element #500 in a million-element array is just as fast as accessing #1. The time doesn't depend on the array size — that's O(1).'

  BST Search O(log n): 'Each comparison eliminates half the remaining nodes. For 1 million nodes, you need at most ~20 comparisons (log2(1,000,000) ≈ 20). Double the data? Just ONE more comparison.'

  Hash Table O(1) avg: 'No matter how many entries you have — 100 or 100 million — finding a key takes the same time on average. That's the magic of hashing.'

  Array Insert O(n): 'Inserting at position 0 means shifting every single element. Double the array? Double the work. That's what O(n) means.'
acceptanceCriteria: Every DS has a complexity intuition paragraph in properties panel
Explains what the O notation means in concrete terms
Uses specific numbers ('1 million elements → ~20 comparisons')
Explains how doubling input affects performance
Language is conversational, not mathematical

title: Add real-world connection hooks for all 34 data structures
description:
Most topics score 0-1 on T9 (Real-World Connection). Students don't know WHERE these are used.

For each DS, add specific production system references:
- Array: 'Every variable in JavaScript is backed by a V8 array'
- Hash Table: 'Redis, Python dict, browser localStorage, DNS resolution'
- BST: 'File system directory indexing (old ext2)'
- AVL: 'Database indexes in older systems'
- Red-Black Tree: 'Java TreeMap, C++ std::map, Linux CFS scheduler'
- Heap: 'Operating system process scheduler, Dijkstra's algorithm'
- Trie: 'Phone keyboard autocomplete, DNS resolution, IP routing'
- Bloom Filter: 'Chrome Safe Browsing, Google BigTable, Medium's recommendation dedup'
- LSM Tree: 'LevelDB, RocksDB, Cassandra, CockroachDB'
- Consistent Hash: 'Amazon DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra, CDN load balancing'
- Skip List: 'Redis sorted sets (ZRANGEBYSCORE)'
- Merkle Tree: 'Git version control, Bitcoin blockchain, IPFS'

Source: Engineering blogs from Google, AWS re:Invent 2025, Redis docs.
acceptanceCriteria: Every DS has at least 2 specific real-world system references
References name specific products (Redis, PostgreSQL, Chrome)
One-line explanation of WHY that system chose this DS
Visible in properties panel as 'Used In' section

title: Add memorable 3-bullet summary for each data structure
description:
Zero topics have a summary or takeaway (T12 = 0 for all). After exploring a DS, the student has no crisp mental anchor.

For each DS, add a 3-bullet 'Key Takeaways' card at the bottom of the properties panel:

Hash Table:
  1. O(1) average lookup — just compute hash, go to bucket
  2. Collisions are the enemy — chaining or probing to handle them
  3. Load factor > 0.75? Time to resize (double the buckets)

BST:
  1. Left < Parent < Right — this rule enables binary search on a tree
  2. O(log n) average, but O(n) if tree degenerates to a line
  3. Self-balancing variants (AVL, RB) prevent degeneration

Bloom Filter:
  1. 'Definitely NOT in set' is always right. 'Probably in set' might be wrong.
  2. More hash functions (k) = fewer false positives, but more bits set
  3. You can add elements but NEVER remove them (bits can't be unset)

These should be copy-pasteable to flashcards.
acceptanceCriteria: Every DS has a 3-bullet 'Key Takeaways' section
Each bullet is one concise sentence (flashcard-ready)
Bullets capture the ESSENCE — not just facts but INSIGHT
Visible at bottom of properties panel
Copy-to-clipboard button for flashcard export

title: Add common mistakes and misconceptions for top 15 data structures
description:
Zero topics address common errors (T8 = 0 for all). Students repeat the same mistakes every generation.

Based on Reddit threads, StackOverflow, and teaching experience:

Hash Table mistakes:
  - 'Hash tables are always O(1)' → Wrong, worst case is O(n) with many collisions
  - 'Any object can be a key' → Only immutable/hashable objects
  - 'Hash tables maintain insertion order' → Only in some implementations (Python 3.7+, LinkedHashMap)

BST mistakes:
  - 'BST search is always O(log n)' → Only if balanced; sorted insert → O(n)
  - 'Deleting a node with two children is simple' → Need in-order successor
  - 'Left child < parent' → Actually left child ≤ parent in some implementations

Linked List mistakes:
  - 'Linked lists are faster than arrays' → Only for insert at head; arrays win on access
  - 'Following pointers is free' → Cache misses make LL traversal much slower than array

Source: Reddit r/learnprogramming, GeeksforGeeks common errors, teaching experience.
acceptanceCriteria: Top 15 DS have at least 2 common mistakes documented
Mistakes framed as 'Students often think X, but actually Y'
Each mistake includes WHY it's wrong
Visible in properties panel as 'Common Mistakes' section
Mistakes verified against authoritative sources

title: Add edge case demonstrations for top 10 data structures
description:
Zero topics demonstrate edge cases (T6 = 0 for all). Edge cases are the most powerful teaching tool — they reveal the DS's true nature.

Add preset buttons for key edge cases:

BST: 'Sorted Input' button → inserts [1,2,3,4,5] showing degeneration to linked list
BST: 'Balanced Input' → inserts [4,2,6,1,3,5,7] showing perfect balance
Hash Table: 'All Same Hash' → inserts keys that all map to bucket 0, showing worst-case chaining
Heap: 'Already Sorted' → builds heap from sorted array, showing O(n) build
Bloom Filter: 'Fill It Up' → inserts many items showing FP rate climbing
AVL: 'Trigger All Rotations' → sequence that triggers LL, RR, LR, RL rotations

Each edge case has a description explaining WHAT it demonstrates and WHY it matters.
acceptanceCriteria: Top 10 DS have at least 2 preset edge case buttons
Each edge case has a label and explanation
BST degeneration case shows height = n (linked list shape)
Hash table collision case shows O(n) chain length
AVL rotation cases show all 4 rotation types
Edge cases teach something about the DS's properties

title: Add comparison guides between related data structures
description:
Zero topics compare with alternatives (T7 = 0 for all). The most common student question is 'When should I use X vs Y?'

Add comparison sections:
  BST vs AVL vs Red-Black: 'BST is simplest but can degenerate. AVL is strictly balanced (faster lookups). Red-Black is approximately balanced (faster inserts). Use AVL for read-heavy, RB for write-heavy.'
  Array vs Linked List: 'Array for random access, LL for frequent insertions at head/middle.'
  Hash Table vs BST: 'Hash table for O(1) unordered lookup. BST for sorted operations and range queries.'
  B-Tree vs B+ Tree: 'B+ Tree for databases (range queries via leaf chain). B-Tree for general-purpose.'
  Heap vs BST: 'Heap for min/max only (O(1) peek). BST for arbitrary search (O(log n)).'
  LSM Tree vs B+ Tree: 'LSM for write-heavy (sequential writes). B+ for read-heavy (in-place updates).'

Show as a 'Compare' tab or expandable section in properties panel.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 8 comparison guides covering major DS families
Each comparison explains WHEN to use each alternative
Trade-offs clearly stated (speed vs space, read vs write, etc.)
Accessible from properties panel 'Compare' section
Links to both DS topics being compared
